content.text: Ideation Workshops for Claude Use Cases

Enterprise Claude programmes that rely on bottom-up idea submission surface the same five use cases everywhere — meeting summaries, email drafting, and report generation. These are real opportunities, but they represent a fraction of the value available. A structured ideation workshop breaks participants out of the familiar and systematically surfaces the higher-leverage, less obvious use cases that tend to generate the strongest ROI.

This document provides a complete facilitation guide for a two-hour Claude use case ideation workshop, including the session flow, materials, facilitation techniques, common failure modes, and the canvas template that converts raw ideas into structured requirements for the feasibility assessment stage.

attrs.alt: Claude use case ideation workshop flow: five phases over two hours from context setting through pain storming, idea generation, prioritisation, and next steps

content.text: Why Standard Brainstorming Fails for AI Use Cases
Generic brainstorming sessions consistently under-generate AI use cases for two structural reasons:
The blank-page problem:

Without concrete knowledge of what Claude can and cannot do, participants anchor on vague ideas ('make our reports better') that cannot be scoped or evaluated. The workshop must first build a shared, accurate mental model of Claude's capabilities before ideation begins.
The automation-first bias:

Participants default to thinking about tasks that Claude might fully automate, skipping the much larger category of tasks where Claude can meaningfully augment human work. Augmentation use cases are often faster to deploy, lower risk, and higher impact at comparable effort — but they require prompting the group to think differently about the human-AI boundary.

A structured workshop with the right materials, sequence, and facilitation corrects both problems. The key insight is that the session must teach before it ideates — 20 minutes of capability grounding generates three to four times the useful ideas as jumping straight to 'what could Claude do for you?'
Pre-Workshop Preparation
Participant Selection

The ideal ideation group for a single business unit is six to twelve people. Below six, the group lacks the diversity of perspective needed to surface cross-functional use cases. Above twelve, the group dynamics fragment and quieter participants disengage.
Composition matters more than headcount. Include:
Frontline operators (50% of seats):

The people who do the work day-to-day. They know the pain points, the workarounds, and the edge cases that managers summarise away. A proposal writer, a contract reviewer, an onboarding coordinator — not the VP who oversees them.
Process owners (25% of seats):
The leads or managers who understand the full process flow and can assess feasibility from an operational perspective.
Technical liaison (one person):

Someone who understands Claude's capabilities and can answer 'could Claude actually do that?' questions in real time. This is usually a member of the CoE, a platform engineer, or a product manager who has worked with the Claude API.
Neutral facilitator (one person, does not hold a seat):
The facilitator owns the process, not the content. They should have no stake in which ideas win, so they can enforce time-boxing and redirect tangents without political cost.
Pre-Read Materials
Send participants a three-page briefing document 48 hours before the session. Three pages maximum — more is not read. The briefing must contain:

A one-paragraph plain-language description of what Claude is: a large language model accessed via API, capable of reading, writing, analysing, and transforming text — not a search engine, not a database query tool, not a numeric calculator
Seven capability trigger cards (one per domain) with two to three enterprise examples each — these become the physical or digital cards used in Phase 3
Two concrete examples from similar organisations or departments: one quick win (low effort, delivered value within 30 days) and one strategic bet (higher effort, longer timeline, larger impact)

One explicit 'what Claude cannot do well' list — numerical computation, real-time data retrieval, tasks requiring external system action without an API integration, tasks where output errors are catastrophic and there is no human review step

The 'what Claude cannot do well' list is as important as the capability trigger cards. Workshops that skip it generate a flood of ideas that fail feasibility screening — wasting the team's post-workshop energy on ideas that were never viable.
Facilitation Materials

For in-person sessions: sticky notes (three colours: one for pains, one for ideas, one for concerns), dot stickers for voting (five per person), a 2×2 matrix drawn on a whiteboard, and printed capability trigger cards.

For remote sessions: a Miro or Figjam board pre-populated with the 2×2 matrix, sticky note templates, and the capability trigger cards as embedded images. Use anonymous sticky notes during pain-storming to reduce anchoring to whoever speaks first.
Workshop Phase Guide
Phase 1: Context Setting (15 minutes)

The facilitator opens with a five-minute walkthrough of the relevant value stream map, focusing on the two or three process steps identified in the capability heat map as high-fit for this BU. This grounds ideation in real process pain rather than abstract possibilities.

After the walkthrough, the technical liaison delivers a ten-minute capability overview using the trigger cards. For each domain, they give one concrete example and one explicit counter-example. The goal is not to be comprehensive — it is to create a shared vocabulary ('that's a summarisation use case' / 'that would need data extraction first') that the group will use for the rest of the session.
Phase 2: Pain Storming (30 minutes)

Pain storming is silent writing before group discussion. Each participant writes their top three process pain points on sticky notes — one pain per note — in eight minutes without talking. The silence prevents anchoring on the first idea spoken aloud and ensures every voice contributes equally.

After the silent write, each participant reads their pains aloud (one minute per person) while placing them on the board. The facilitator affinity-clusters similar pains in real time — typical clusters that emerge:
Document creation overhead (proposals, reports, summaries)
Context-switching to find information (policy lookup, precedent research, CRM hunting)
Coordination and handoff failures (briefing gaps, incomplete handovers, rework from miscommunication)
Inconsistency in output quality (depends on who does the task, on what day, with what context)
First-draft anxiety (paralysis at the blank page, particularly for non-writers in writing-intensive roles)
Close Phase 2 with a five-dot vote: each participant places dots on the pain clusters that represent the highest business impact. The top five pain clusters are the focus for Phase 3.
Phase 3: Idea Generation (30 minutes)

For each of the top five pain clusters, the facilitator poses the question: 'How might Claude address this pain?' The team uses the capability trigger cards as prompts. Each idea goes on a new sticky note. The rules of this phase:
No evaluation during ideation — 'that won't work because...' is not allowed
Build on others' ideas with '+1' notes that extend rather than replace
Wild ideas are welcome — they can be refined in prioritisation
Quantity over quality: aim for at least 20 ideas in 30 minutes
Common high-value ideas that workshops surface when run correctly:
Pain Cluster
Common Obvious Ideas (surface quickly)
Less Obvious High-Value Ideas (emerge with prompting)
Document creation overhead
Auto-draft reports, auto-draft emails
Generate the first-draft briefing from meeting notes before the next meeting; create a library of approved reusable components that Claude assembles to order
Context-switching for information
Internal chatbot for FAQs
Claude as a 'briefing compiler' that reads three upstream systems and produces a one-page situational brief before every customer call
Coordination and handoff failures
Auto-generate handover notes
Claude detects gap in a handover document and prompts the sender to complete missing fields before submitting
Output quality inconsistency
Standardise with templates
Claude scores its own output against a rubric before delivery, surfacing the score to the human reviewer to focus attention on low-confidence sections
First-draft anxiety
Claude writes the first draft
Claude provides a structured outline with section starters that preserve the writer's voice while eliminating the blank-page moment
Phase 4: Prioritisation (25 minutes)

The facilitator moves all ideas onto the 2×2 impact–effort matrix — either physically or digitally. Each participant gets five dot stickers (or digital votes) to place on the ideas they believe will generate the highest business impact relative to implementation effort.

attrs.alt: Use case prioritisation matrix showing quick wins, strategic bets, fill-ins, and reconsider quadrants with example use cases plotted by impact and effort

content.text: After voting, the facilitator leads a ten-minute discussion on the top three ideas — those with the most votes. Key questions for each:
What data or systems would Claude need access to in order to execute this?
Who is the human reviewer in this workflow, and what would they need to verify?
What is the smallest version of this use case that could be piloted in two weeks?

Close Phase 4 by labelling the top three ideas with: a provisional use case name, an owner from the room who will lead the feasibility assessment, and a confidence level (high / medium / low) from the technical liaison on whether Claude can plausibly do this.
Phase 5: Next Steps (20 minutes)
The final phase converts prioritised ideas into structured artefacts. Each of the top three ideas gets a one-page use case canvas completed in the room:

# Use Case Canvas

**Use Case Name**: [One sentence, verb-noun form — e.g. "Compile pre-call customer briefing"]

**Value Stream**: [Which of the five streams does this sit in?]
**Process Step**: [Which specific step in that stream?]

**Pain Addressed**: [Copy the pain cluster sticky note text verbatim]

**What Claude Does**:
- Input: [what content/data Claude receives]
- Transformation: [summarise / draft / extract / classify / analyse]
- Output: [what Claude produces — format, length, structure]

**Human Role**:
- Review: [what the human checks before use]
- Edit: [what the human typically adjusts]
- Approve: [who gives final sign-off]

**Deployment Mode**: [ ] Augmentation  [ ] Automation (specify scope if automation)

**Data Requirements**:
- Sources needed: [CRM / email / internal doc store / external?]
- Data sensitivity: [ ] Tier 1 (open) [ ] Tier 2 (confidential) [ ] Tier 3 (regulated)

**Success Metric**:
- Primary: [e.g. time to produce briefing — baseline: 45 min, target: 10 min]
- Secondary: [e.g. rep satisfaction with briefing quality]

**Owner**: [Name, role]
**Feasibility Review Date**: [within two weeks of this workshop]

The canvas is not a requirements document — it is a structured handoff to the feasibility assessment stage. The owner takes the canvas as their input for the technical and data-readiness assessment that determines whether the use case can proceed to pilot.
Common Workshop Failure Modes
Failure Mode
How It Presents
Prevention
HIPPO effect (Highest Paid Person's Opinion dominates)
Senior leaders speak first; room defers to their framing; frontline knowledge is suppressed
Silent write in Phase 2 before any verbal sharing; facilitator enforces equal airtime; anonymous digital voting
Capability overestimation
Ideas require real-time data, external system actions, or sub-1% error rates that Claude cannot reliably deliver
Technical liaison attends Phase 3 and flags low-feasibility ideas with a yellow sticky note (not rejection, just 'needs technical review')
Session runs out of time before canvases are complete
Good ideas are captured as bullet points; no structured handoff exists; momentum is lost post-workshop
Time-box Phases 2–4 strictly; Phase 5 canvas completion is non-negotiable; schedule 30 min buffer for the first workshop
Only obvious ideas surface
All ideas are in the 'email drafting / meeting summary' category; nothing domain-specific emerges
Use domain-specific capability trigger cards prepared from the BU's value stream steps; ask 'what did you do in the first hour of your day last Tuesday?'
No follow-through after the workshop
Canvases are filed; no feasibility reviews happen; team feels exercise was performative
Confirm feasibility review dates in the room before closing; the facilitator emails the review calendar invites within 24 hours of the workshop
Scaling Ideation Across Multiple Business Units
Running the same workshop structure across all BUs in sequence is the most thorough approach but can take months in a large enterprise. Three acceleration patterns for scaling ideation across BUs:
Hub-and-spoke cascade:

Run one deep ideation workshop per business function (Sales, Legal, HR, Finance), then use the output as a reference catalogue for sub-function workshops. Sub-function sessions can be 60 minutes rather than two hours because they start from a pre-populated idea list and focus on qualifying and adapting ideas to their specific context.
Cross-BU ideation day:

Combine participants from three to five BUs in a single full-day session. Mixed-BU groups surface cross-functional use cases — particularly integration use cases where Claude automates the handoff between two departments — that single-BU sessions miss entirely.
Asynchronous ideation:

For distributed teams, run Phases 1–2 asynchronously (participants complete the pre-read and submit pain points via a shared form), then hold a 90-minute virtual session for Phases 3–5 only. This approach loses some spontaneity but dramatically reduces scheduling friction.
Capability Trigger Cards: The Ideation Accelerator

Capability trigger cards are the single most impactful facilitation material for Claude use case workshops. Each card describes one Claude capability domain, shows two enterprise examples, and poses a trigger question that participants answer for their own context. Prepare these cards before every workshop and customise the examples to the BU.
Capability Domain
What It Does
Example 1
Example 2
Trigger Question
Text Generation
Creates original prose from instructions, templates, or source material
Sales: First-draft proposal section given opportunity notes
HR: Job description from role competencies spreadsheet
What documents does your team produce repeatedly that follow a similar structure each time?
Summarisation
Condenses long content into structured key points
Legal: Executive summary of 40-page contract for non-lawyer stakeholders
Finance: Weekly commentary on variance analysis from 20-page report
What do you receive that takes too long to read, understand, or act on?
Data Extraction
Finds and structures specific information from unstructured text
Procurement: Extracts payment terms, renewal dates, and penalty clauses from supplier contracts
Customer Service: Pulls issue type, product, and root cause from support tickets
What information do you look up manually in documents, emails, or systems to fill a form or make a decision?
Q&A / Knowledge Retrieval
Answers questions from a provided document or knowledge base
HR: Answers employee policy questions from the company handbook
Compliance: Answers 'does this contract clause comply with our standard?' against approved contract playbook
What questions do your team members ask you repeatedly that you answer the same way each time?
Code Generation
Writes, explains, and reviews code and scripts
Engineering: Generates boilerplate API integration code from an OpenAPI spec
Data: Writes SQL queries for common analytical questions against documented schema
What repetitive coding tasks consume time that could be templated?
Classification
Labels inputs according to a taxonomy
Support: Routes tickets to the correct team by issue category
Finance: Tags expenses to the correct GL code from receipt description
What incoming items (emails, tickets, documents, records) need to be sorted, tagged, or routed every day?
Decision Support
Synthesises options and surfaces recommendations from evidence
Procurement: Recommends preferred vendor from scoring matrix and qualitative notes
Talent Acquisition: Provides hire / no-hire recommendation summary from interview feedback
Where do you make repeated decisions that follow a consistent framework but still require pulling together scattered information?

Print these as A5 cards (one card per table of participants). In remote sessions, embed them as sticky note clusters on the Miro/Figjam board. Participants reference the trigger question during Phase 3 when a pain cluster is not obviously connected to a capability domain.
Designing for Divergent and Convergent Thinking

Effective ideation workshops alternate deliberately between divergent and convergent thinking. The session flow described above is structured to honour this rhythm — failing to maintain the rhythm is one of the most common facilitation mistakes:
Phase
Thinking Mode
Facilitator Role
Common Mistake
Phase 1: Context Setting
Neutral / Preparatory
Inform, not inspire — facts only
Overpromising what Claude can do, inflating expectations
Phase 2: Pain Storming
Divergent
Create silence, resist urge to comment, cluster without editorialising
Allowing discussion before all pains are written — anchors the group to early ideas
Phase 3: Idea Generation
Divergent (maximally)
Restate the no-evaluation rule whenever someone starts to critique; use trigger cards to unblock stuck participants
Letting the group self-edit — 'that won't work' collapses idea count
Phase 4: Prioritisation
Convergent
Enforce the 2×2 framework; prevent debate about methodology; use dot votes, not committee decisions
Spending too long debating individual ideas rather than using the voting mechanism
Phase 5: Canvases
Structured output
Assign canvas owner; ensure each canvas has all fields completed; book feasibility reviews before room disperses
Leaving canvases incomplete — rough notes are not canvases; they will not survive handoff

The hardest facilitation move is enforcing divergence during idea generation. When a participant says 'that won't work because Claude can't access our CRM', the correct response is not to agree or disagree — it is: 'Write it down anyway. We'll assess feasibility in Phase 4.' Feasibility screening in Phase 3 kills ideas before they are fully formed.
Measuring Workshop Effectiveness
Not all workshops are equally productive. Track these four measures across workshops to identify what is working and where facilitation or materials need improvement:
Idea count:

The total number of ideas generated across all pain clusters. A healthy workshop generates 20–40 ideas in Phase 3. Below 15 suggests participants arrived without enough capability context; above 50 often means ideas are too vague to be canvased.
Use case canvas completion rate:

The percentage of top-3 ideas that have a completed canvas within 48 hours of the workshop. Target 100%. Below 80% indicates that Phase 5 was rushed or that the owner assignment was not confirmed clearly.
Feasibility review attendance:

The percentage of use case owners who complete their feasibility review within two weeks of the workshop date. Below 70% indicates organisational friction or unclear expectations about what a feasibility review entails.
Pilot conversion rate:

The percentage of workshopped ideas that progress to a pilot within 90 days. A well-run programme typically converts 20–30% of workshopped ideas to pilots (not all ideas should pilot — screening is healthy). Below 10% may indicate that workshops are surfacing ideas that consistently fail feasibility; above 40% may indicate insufficient feasibility filtering.
Post-Workshop Handoff Protocol
The 24 hours after a workshop determine whether the energy generated converts into forward motion. A specific handoff protocol prevents the common collapse between ideation and execution:
Within 2 hours of the session:

The facilitator photographs all sticky notes and the 2×2 matrix (physical sessions) or exports the Miro/Figjam board (remote sessions) and saves them in the programme's shared drive with a timestamp and participant list.
Within 24 hours:

The facilitator sends a summary email to all participants with: the pain cluster list, all ideas captured (not just the top 3), the prioritised top 3 with their canvas owners, and the confirmed feasibility review dates.
Within 48 hours:

Each canvas owner completes their use case canvas and shares it with the technical liaison for a preliminary feasibility flag (10-minute async review — not a full assessment, just a confidence level: high/medium/low/needs-more-info).
Within 2 weeks:

Formal feasibility reviews occur. The canvas owner, the technical liaison, and a platform engineer (15–30 minutes each) review the canvas against the feasibility criteria. Canvases that pass move to the pilot intake queue; those that need rework return with specific gaps identified; those that do not pass are archived with a documented rationale.

Organisations that implement this protocol consistently find that 60–70% of workshopped ideas are still alive at the two-week mark — compared to under 20% in programmes without a structured handoff. The gap is entirely in follow-through discipline, not idea quality.
Domain-Specific Facilitation Tips
Different business units bring different facilitation challenges to the ideation room. Knowing these in advance lets the facilitator prepare targeted adjustments:
Legal and Compliance Teams

Legal teams are trained to identify risks, not generate possibilities. The pain-storming phase typically runs well — they are accustomed to enumerating problems. The idea generation phase often stalls because every idea is immediately met with a risk counter-argument.
Adjustment:

In Phase 3, use an explicit 'assume the risk is managed' prompt: 'Assume legal hold, data residency, and privilege concerns are addressed. What would you want Claude to do?' Document the risk concerns as a separate sticky note category, but do not allow them to block idea generation. They become requirements in the use case canvas, not blockers in ideation.
Engineering and Technical Teams

Engineering teams tend to jump immediately to implementation — 'we could call the Claude API and...' — rather than staying at the problem level. They generate technically sound ideas but sometimes miss the highest-value opportunities because they optimise for what is easiest to build rather than what would most reduce business pain.
Adjustment:

Require that every idea be articulated as a user story before it goes on the board: 'As a [role], I want to [action] so that [outcome].' This format forces the group to stay anchored to the user's need rather than the implementation approach. Technical ideas that cannot be expressed as a user story are not yet well-formed and should be sent back to the sticky note phase.
Finance Teams

Finance teams often believe their work is too precise or too sensitive for Claude. The concern about precision is partially valid — Claude does not replace numerical computation — but it conflates the numerical layer of finance work with the textual layer, which is substantial: variance commentary, board pack narratives, audit correspondence, forecast assumptions, and policy documentation are all high-fit targets.
Adjustment:

Open Phase 1 with an explicit distinction: 'Claude is not doing your models — your models stay in Excel or SAP. Claude is handling the text that surrounds and explains those models. The CFO deck narrative, the variance write-up, the investor FAQ — that is where the opportunity is.' This reframe typically unlocks three to five high-value ideas that the Finance team had previously self-filtered out.
From Ideation to a Living Use Case Backlog

A single workshop is an event; the ideation function should be a continuous process. As the Claude programme matures, the workshop cadence should shift from a one-time exercise to a quarterly ritual embedded in each BU's planning cycle:
Quarters 1–2 (launch):
Run two-hour full workshops with all target BUs. Focus is broad discovery — surface the landscape of use cases. All ideas feed the initial prioritisation matrix.
Quarters 3–4 (deepen):

Run 60-minute 'innovation sprints' with BUs that have at least one pilot live. Focus is narrow: 'What is the next use case we should add to the platform that the current pilot has shown us is possible?' These are smaller sessions that extend the backlog based on deployment learnings.
Ongoing (sustain):

Establish a lightweight asynchronous channel (a shared form, a Slack channel, or a Notion database) where any employee can submit a use case idea year-round. Triage submissions weekly in the CoE standup. Ideas that meet a basic viability bar (maps to a supported capability, has a named pain, has a potential owner) enter the backlog for quarterly prioritisation.

The result is a continuously replenishing use case backlog that the programme can draw from as deployment capacity opens up — replacing the 'what should we build next?' paralysis that stalls many AI programmes after their first two or three use cases.
Summary
Structured ideation workshops
are the discovery mechanism that surfaces high-value, domain-specific Claude use cases that informal idea collection consistently misses.
Teach before you ideate: 20 minutes of capability grounding generates three to four times the viable ideas as jumping straight to brainstorming
Use pain storming before solution generation: surface the pain clusters first, then apply 'How might Claude address this?' to each
Include frontline operators at 50% of seats — they hold the domain knowledge that identifies the highest-leverage automation targets
Follow a five-phase structure: Context Setting → Pain Storming → Idea Generation → Prioritisation → Canvases
Close every workshop with completed use case canvases and confirmed feasibility review dates — momentum dies in the gap between ideation and follow-through
Adapt the workshop format for scale: hub-and-spoke cascade for large enterprises, cross-BU days for cross-functional use cases, async for distributed teams

Workshops that follow this structure consistently produce ten to twenty use case ideas per session, of which three to five pass feasibility assessment and two to three reach pilot within 90 days. The structured format is the difference between a one-time creative exercise and a repeatable discovery engine that continuously feeds the Claude deployment pipeline.
